approach: Dispatch the task with talk send, then run a background ping-poll loop so the driver's foreground stays free; once the loop exits (worker idle), capture the tail with talk read — large enough to catch the <<<RESULT ... RESULT>>> block at the end of ~100 lines of per-package output.

commands.cmd: talk send %42 "Run the full integration test suite (go test ./...) against the current wallet service implementation and identify any regressions. For each regression, give test name, failure mode, and suspected root cause."
commands.purpose: Dispatch the task to the worker Claude in pane %42 via a single atomic paste+Enter.

commands.cmd: until talk ping %42 >/dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 15; done
commands.purpose: Poll the worker's title indicator until it flips back to idle (ping exit 0). Sleep 15s between probes — worker takes ~5 min, so ~20 cheap probes is plenty and cheaper than a tight loop. Run in background so the driver's foreground session stays available for other conversations during the wait.

Once the background poll finishes (notification fires), grab the tail of the worker's pane. 200 lines comfortably covers the ~100-line per-package stream plus the final <<<RESULT ... RESULT>>> report block so the regression findings land in the driver's context.

rationale: talk ping is the canonical idle/busy probe per the skill manual, and the manual's 'Dispatch a task to another Claude and wait for the answer' pattern is exactly this shape: send, poll-until-idle in background, then read. Backgrounding the poll satisfies the 'don't block foreground' requirement — the user can interleave other work, and the Bash completion notification signals when to read. A 200-line read window is safely above the fixture's ~116 visible lines, so the <<<RESULT>>> block (the actual deliverable) is guaranteed to be inside tmux's scrollback window when captured. A file-handoff approach was considered but rejected: adds a second coordination surface (asking the worker to write a file, then reading it) when the pane scrollback already contains a self-delimited RESULT block — strictly more moving parts for the same outcome.
